content:
# Revelation Chapter 17 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 289-290 | **Source Image:** REV_017_page_001.jpg, REV_017_page_002.jpg

**1.** Then one of the seven angels who carried the seven bowls came and spoke to me: "Come here, and I will show you the sentencing of the great prostitute who sits enthroned over many waters --

**2.** the one with whom the kings of the earth have committed sexual immorality, and the inhabitants of the earth have been made drunk on the wine of her debauchery."

**3.** He carried me away in the Spirit into a wilderness, and there I saw a woman sitting on a scarlet beast covered with blasphemous names, having seven heads and ten horns.

**4.** The woman was dressed in purple and scarlet, glittering with gold, precious stones, and pearls. She held in her hand a golden cup overflowing with the disgusting filth of her sexual immorality.

**5.** On her forehead was written a name -- a mystery: BABYLON THE GREAT, MOTHER OF PROSTITUTES AND OF EVERY ABOMINATION ON EARTH.

**6.** I saw that the woman was intoxicated -- drunk on the blood of God's holy people, the blood of those who bore witness to Jesus. When I saw her, I was utterly astonished.

**7.** The angel said to me, "Why are you so astonished? I will explain to you the mystery of the woman and of the beast she rides, the one with seven heads and ten horns.

**8.** The beast you saw once existed, does not exist now, and is about to rise from the bottomless pit -- only to go to its destruction. The inhabitants of the earth whose names have not been written in the Book of Life since the foundation of the world will be astounded when they see the beast, because it once was, then was not, and yet will come again.

**9.** This calls for a mind with wisdom: The seven heads represent seven hills on which the woman sits enthroned.

**10.** They also represent seven kings. Five have already fallen, one currently reigns, and the last has not yet come -- but when he does arrive, he must remain only a short time.

**11.** As for the beast that once existed but now does not -- he himself is an eighth king, yet he belongs to the seven, and he is heading for destruction.

**12.** The ten horns you saw represent ten kings who have not yet received their kingdoms, but who will receive authority to rule as kings alongside the beast for one brief hour.

**13.** These kings are united in purpose and will hand over their power and authority to the beast.

**14.** They will wage war against the Lamb, but the Lamb will triumph over them, because he is Lord of lords and King of kings -- and those who are with him are the called, the chosen, and the faithful."

**15.** Then the angel said to me, "The waters you saw, where the prostitute sits enthroned, represent peoples, multitudes, nations, and languages.

**16.** The ten horns you saw, along with the beast, will come to hate the prostitute. They will strip her bare, devour her flesh, and burn her with fire.

**17.** For God has put it into their hearts to carry out his purpose by agreeing to give their royal authority to the beast, until God's words are fulfilled.

**18.** The woman you saw is the great city that exercises dominion over the kings of the earth."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

In this chapter I have made several dynamic choices to convey the force of John's apocalyptic vision to contemporary readers:

- **"Sentencing" (v. 1):** The Greek *krima* carries the sense of a judicial verdict already rendered, not merely "judgment" in an abstract sense. I chose "sentencing" to convey that the outcome is decided -- what follows is the execution of a verdict.

- **"Sits enthroned over many waters" (v. 1):** The verb *kathemenes* ("sitting") over waters conveys sovereign dominion, not mere geography. "Enthroned" captures this regal posture that the text intends.

- **"Overflowing with the disgusting filth" (v. 4):** The Greek *gemon bdelygmaton* ("full of abominations") is intensified here because the image is deliberately revolting -- a golden cup that looks magnificent on the outside but contains moral sewage.

- **"Utterly astonished" (v. 6):** The Sinaiticus reading *ethaumasa thauma mega* is a cognate construction (literally "I marveled a great marvel") that demands more than just "I wondered." John is staggered by what he sees.

- **"One brief hour" (v. 12):** The Greek *mian horan* is literally "one hour," but in apocalyptic idiom this signals extreme brevity. Adding "brief" makes the rhetorical point explicit for English readers.

- **"Strip her bare, devour her flesh" (v. 16):** These verbs (*eremosin*, *phagosin*) are viscerally violent in the Greek. I have preserved their shocking physicality rather than softening them, because John intends his audience to feel the horror of self-consuming evil.

-- PNEUMA
